Are we live? Yeah. We're all live. Hey, guys, Jordan Belpont here. Weekly intensive. I got a special surprise. So I said yesterday, I said, um, the top, it's going to be a surprise. Well, here's the surprise. This is the weekend that, you know, every, twice a year I do a really ultra high end mastermind with people very successful that, you know, already have made a ton of money. Uh, and then I look at it, take it to the next level. And the great thing about a mastermind is it's not so much that I'm just here training it's really everyone here has expertise and has a unique outlook based on the things they've done in their life where they live, the market they cater to. And so much of, of, of about a mastermind group is not even what happens at the mastermind. It's about what happens after the mastermind, the relationship that you make there, the networking opportunities and so forth. So, um, we just started, we're here in a, Beverly Hills, a good friend of mine, got him Roman James. He's, he's that number one developer in the world probably number one. He builds houses for the ultra rich. This particular house, which he built is on the market for like $45 million. And I'll probably take you for a little tour later, you know, separately after this and just a quick to show you then later this afternoon, he's taking us all to another house he built, which is I think the world's Roman's most expensive house in the world right now. And like, it's like, you can move in, he built it. You can move in. You need to fucking toothbrushes. All you need basically everything else is there. Okay. They even have someone that blows nothing. No, but no, but it, hey, hey, hey, hey, Roman wanted that. He was scared. It wasn't politically correct. Okay. But anyway, no, but the house is, um, comes with cars. Like it comes a helicopter. You never see anything like it. It's fucking insane. Anyway, um, so what I want to do today is I want to, I want to make today a little special because I thought it would be really good to have some other people that could answer your questions to some very special people here. Right. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to go about, I'm going to take about 40, 50% of, no, not 60% of this training. I want to cover a topic that I think is just one of the most important things you can ever learn doing your life. And that is how to tell stories. The art of telling a great story. Um, and I know it'll be very relevant to everyone who's here today with me here, you know, at this home and also, no matter who you are, what you do for a living and even outside of business, if this goes beyond business, let me take this one for business, for selling, for persuading, the ability to tell a great story is like one of them. It's one of the most important things you could ever learn to do beyond. Let's put closing aside, because if you can't fucking close your fucking or work with moron, we should just leave the planet earth, right? But the point is beyond the simple obvious truth that the straight line is showing you how to close, there's another aspect to communication. And that is the art of just being interesting. The art of being able to hold someone's attention. The art of being able to take someone who's a customer and really get into a much deeper level of rapport. Because remember, the rules of the game of the straight line are like, you know, you don't want to go up to Pluto before someone's a customer. Now, obviously with a very long sales cycle, it's super big. Players, a little bit different. But the point is in a regular traditional sales cycle, you want to keep the line of questioning relevant to conversation relevant. But once you close the person, then it's all about going outside that into, you know, that person of life, what makes them feel alive outside of work. And also when you're working on bigger accounts with longer sales cycle, sales cycle, what you're facing, you're going to have to be not so much going off to Pluto, but you're going to have to tell stories to people about your life and also have, in a way that brings them back into the game. In other words, when I found out there in a business setting telling stories, I want to make sure that I'm not just telling stories for the sake of telling stories. I'm doing it with an outcome in mind. I'm framing a certain point. And then I'm using it. By which camera am I supposed to be looking at? Which one is the main camera? Oh, good. I feel like I'm looking in the wrong fucking camera. Like, it's you. I'm looking at it. So anyway, so I guess one of the things, you know, everyone's got a certain type of chair. I have a different chair from my back here, right? But I'm in this shitty chair that reclines. Anyway, one of the things that I've, it's a natural gift I've had since I'm a kid, right? I could tell a fucking story like there's no one else's business. In fact, one of the people here today, Kyle, said he said, the funniest stories ever heard in his life was the whole YouTube story I told, right? I don't know if you guys have all heard this, right? But, you know, I tell a story about the yacht sinking, right? And I told, that was actually a tape that was made. It's not, you check it out if it's really funny. And it's basically some animations accompanying my story, right? But the story itself, you know, when I tell the story, for me, it's very effortless. And any time I tell a story, it's just effortless for me. And people laugh and they're engaged. And I hear other people tell stories sometimes, they kind of suck at it. Like, their stories just are interesting. And it's not because they haven't lived interesting lives. You get it? It's not because they don't have interesting stories to tell. They just don't know how to tell them. And so, let's just two-possess. Number one is how do you just fucking tell a story to be interesting? So you can communicate with people like, you know, opposite sex, same sex, whatever your personal age is. I don't give a shit which way you go, okay? Number two is how do you attract human beings to want to essentially engage with you, right? Storytelling is the oldest form of communication. And it's really all goes back to the campfire. The reason that storytelling is so effective in selling is that it goes back to how we learned in the caveman days. You're sitting around a campfire and information was passed out to the telling of stories. If you look at the, what's the best-selling book of all time? What? The Bible. It's one giant story, right? Now, some people say it's true and some say it's not. Some say it's loose and temperate. There's not a point. It's one hell of a fucking story, right? So, when I was going out there and started doing trainings, this is back in 2010 when I first started really going out formally into the world and doing this stuff, right? I always done it for my own businesses, but I started training other people. And I started getting this, you know, people saying, like, God, your story is so funny. It's so great. You're laughing so hard. Can you tell this story? Can you tell that story, right? And I almost felt like I was, and then even my own friend, when I wrote my book, they're like, you know, people like me, can you tell me this story? And they'd be rolling. And then I started realizing that when people would tell their stories, they're kind of like sucked. You know, and I can say, is it just because I've lived that insane a life, or maybe I have, but it's not just that. It's the way I tell it. So, I started saying to myself, okay, well, maybe, let me try to do the same thing I did with the straight line system is let me actually dissect this. First of all, do I have a strategy for telling stories? I started thinking about, like, is there a formula I use? Because what happens typically is things that you do really, really well, you're using a strategy, you're just doing it unconsciously. You don't think about it. So, there was some, for some, whatever it was, I, my brain was widened, certainly, that I naturally cracked code for telling a great story. And I'm not the only great storyteller. There's great storytellers out there in the world, right? So, the point is, but my, I come up with a formula to telling a story, and it allowed me to make even things that weren't that interesting, really interesting. My wife says, you know, honestly, the way you talk to them is that you could read the fucking phone book and you make it sound interesting. Right? So, and, you know, I, I assure you, I don't read the phone book every night, but, but I, I can do that. And there's a reason why. So, I actually spent some time a bunch of years ago, and I figured out what I was doing. And I actually made it into a strategy, like a step-by-step strategy. And then I put a formula around it for business. So, it shows you a, how to tell a story to get a certain point across in business, to frame a certain point, right? So, you could use this strategy both for, you know, in your own life, just to be interesting and funny when you want to tell a story in a crowd or in a social engagement, right? Or you could use it to frame a certain point in business, right? So, I'm going to first go through, and I broke it down here. I think altogether, I think I have eight steps here. Let me just make sure there are eight steps. One, two, three, there's eight steps, okay? So, eight steps. I'm going to go through eight steps first, all eight steps, all right? And then I'm going to actually tell you a story myself and just watch how I do it, okay? And then what we'll probably do is, depending on, you know, I can fucking talk. I know that. So, if we have time, maybe one of these guys will tell a story here, and I'll show what they do wrong. Because when I do this in live seminars where, like, I'll teach the art of storytelling and everyone gets it. I call them on stage, and when they try to do it, they make really blaring errors. And if I correct them just a little bit, within like 15 minutes, they're telling amazing stories. So, we'll see if we can get to that. If not, you can do this on your own and just like start telling your story and tape yourself, and then you can actually see the mistakes that you make. So, let's go through the points one by one, okay? It starts off, is that what you want to do is, in a business setting, okay, you frame the point first. I'll give you an example here. One of the stories I always tell is about something that I'm going to cover here this weekend is state management. And you guys, I've covered this last week, in fact, how to trigger a peak state, right, with anchoring. So, again, just to refresh everyone's recollection, state management is the ability to trigger an empowered state at will, regardless of what's going on you in the environment. So, in other words, you know, you could be having a terrible day. You could be feeling negative inside. You could be feeling uncertain about things. You're not feeling overly confident. You could even be feeling overwhelmed, but you're about to enter a situation of influence, and you have that ability in a moment's notice to trigger a state of absolute certainty, of confidence, of clarity, to the point where you feel it. That's state management, right? And I have to tell you that the ability to manage your state is one of the most crucial things that you can master when it comes to selling. Because in the absence of that, you really can't sell the world. Because remember what I always said to you, now, what I'm doing here is I'm doing this on purpose here. I'm framing a point here. What I'm doing is I'm framing a point, which is that state management is fucking crucial. You must know how to do it, and if you don't know how to do it, it's going to really impede you from being your top closer because here's the deal. Because at the highest level, sales is the transference of emotion, right? Here's my point. My main thesis at the highest level, sales is the transference of emotion. And the primary emotion that you're transferring is what? Certainty. Certainty that your product is the best, that it's perfect for them. Certainty that you're someone they can trust and connect with. Certainty that your company is someone they can trust. They're like the three tens, right? And as I always say, the certainty doesn't come from the straight line. The straight line doesn't create certainty. It transfers certainty. The certainty has to come from within you. Straight line doesn't create. That's the mistake people make. So if you walk around in a glum, negative, uncertain state, and you try to use the straight line system, there's no one, there's nothing to transfer. It's almost like the metaphor is I said, imagine a house, right? And your house has got a boiler downstairs or a furnace, and that furnace creates heat, right? And then there's this duct work all throughout the house, the HVAC system, right? And that HVAC system allows the heat to go from the basement to all the rooms in the house, right? So imagine in sales, the straight line system is the duct work and you are the furnace. So if the furnace is broken, you could have the most elaborate, perfectly constructed duct work, copper tubing, insulated, perfect. No heat is dissipated. It goes to every room. So if the furnace is broken, can you heat the rooms? No. You have nothing to transfer. That's why it's so important to be able to manage your state. In fact, let me tell you a story. And here I go. So that's, so I will, when I'm trying to frame a point, I really take the time. The setup is as important as the story. Before I tell you a story, I'm going to tell you a really funny story, which I might have, you may be remembering, I've told it many times, right? But I don't just tell a story. I really hammer home the importance of a certain point because that raises the stakes for the story. And I'll be so serious about the story, about the frame, and then I'll tell a funny fucking story. All right? So now watch this. In fact, let me tell you. Back my first job out of college, you know, I went to dental school for one day, right? I dropped out and then I went and started selling meat and seafood door to door, right? And the first person I meet, they, they, they're a way of training salesman. They put me in a truck with another salesman. His name is the Pegman because he walks like a stick up his ass and everyone starts to laugh, right? The first thing I do is called a pattern interrupt. Okay? So that's it. So let's say number one is I frame a point, right? Number two is I use a pattern interrupt. I say something that's a bit weird or funny or offhanded. In this case, I'm going to tell you a story about a guy who is the penguin. Okay? And the reason he's the penguin is because he walks around like this while and this is going to fucking stick up his ass. So everyone calls him a penguin. Burgess Mary from Batman, nothing on this fucking guy, a day at the veto, right? That's the penguin. So I always, if I tell a story about my yacht sinking, right? I'll say, here's how I tell the story of my yacht sinking. Okay, here's the story. Okay, you all want to know the story, right? Let me just stop by saying this. There are four phases to a Kwe Lut Hai. You know, everyone's like, what? Four phases. And I interrupt the, it's called the pattern interrupt. I start off in a way that's like, what? Everyone, it grabs everyone's attention and it changes their state and makes them, but what's he saying? And I use a pattern interrupt. Okay? So that's step number two. I always start my stories with a pattern interrupt. In this case, I'm telling you a story of state management. My first job, real job out of college after I dropped out of dental school was selling meat and seafood door to door. And I had to train with another salesperson to experience salesperson. That was their system, sitting in a truck with someone else. And this guy's name was the penguin because he had to stick up his ass. He'd wobble around, right? And that's the, so that's like the pattern interrupt to the story, right? The next thing is with stories. Okay? Four phases. One of the things that I like to do is don't be afraid to tell a personal story. This is a big one here. People have a habit of telling stories about other things that don't relate to them. The story will never be as good if you are not in it. And here's the thing. We all have stories that we can find. When I first started speaking publicly, right, to other people, not my own companies, I started thinking, wow, I need to have stories to tell, right? And I started realizing that as I moved through life, everything became a story. So it was like every day there's a story and what goes on in your life. And once you start getting into the habit of telling stories, you'll start saying, you go back and you have a million stories to tell about your life, about the people that you know, the experiences that you've had. I have stories for everything, all right? And I didn't have to make them up or invent experiences. You don't care what you do for living, who you are, what you've done. You have stories to tell. So don't be scared to tell a personal story. Don't just say, oh, let me tell you a story about my friend Joe. It's much more impactful when you're telling a story about yourself in the first person. You're the voyeur looking out and telling the story. So personal stories have a much larger impact than stories that are told about other people. They're just never as good, okay? Number four, tell the story with, this is the big one. This is your first big tip here, ready? Use active dialogue. What I always do when I tell a story is I will get to a certain point in the story and then I switch it into actually telling you the story as if you were there. In other words, watch the story with the penguin, right? So let me tell you a story about state management, right? And my first year of out of college, right? I shoveled driveways, I sold meat, I did ice as well. But the first real job was selling meat and seafood door to door. Boxes of shrimp, lobstails, right? And I walked into this, I answered it and the paper and it was a warehouse full of like 12 guys in these beat up pickup trucks, right? They'd freeze the boxes in the back and they were going door to door just to homes actually and business and selling home freezer plants, right? And their system of training sales was simple. They said, you know, you're going to put you in a truck and with experienced salesmen, so to speak, right? And you just get on mirror them, watch them, what they do all day long and then do it for a day and they'll give you your own truck, right? So they put me with the salesman in the penguin, right? So the penguins like this, you know, they have five foot six, he walked around while with a stick up his ass, right? So I meet the penguin and the first thing the penguin says to me is this. He says, the key to sales and look what I do. Now I go in and I bring you into the story and I say, and the first thing the penguin, so the penguin, he get in the truck and the first thing the penguin says to me, he turns to me and goes, listen, the key to selling is you got to be pumped up and positive. So I'm like, okay, listen, no matter what you do, if they slam the door in your face, no matter how many times they say, no, just always say, haven't I stayed? And like, why is he good? I said, why? Because it makes you feel better about yourself. So I'm like, okay, penguins like, watch, so what I've done now is rather than just saying, I went down, I got in the truck and the penguin said to me, he said to me that, you know, the key was that you had to be, remain pumped and positive and whatever they say to you, I don't say, I say, and the penguin turns to me and he says, so I bring you into the story using active dialogue. So watch, let me do it again, we'll use grammar here. So we get in the truck, right? And the penguin turns to me, not he turned to me and he didn't turn to me and said, that's past tense. So we get inside the truck and the penguin turns to me and he says, now where are you? You're in the present tense with me. I bring you inside the story and I tell the story in the present tense. It dramatically increases their investment in the story. You bring them in to the story. Okay. So I, you'll, anytime I tell a story, you'll notice I'll go so far. And I'll say, and then the yacht was taken. So we all go down there and my wife turns, which says, my bags are here. I'm like, what in the fucking driver forgot the bags? So I said, honey, I said, honey, don't worry about it. I bring you in with active dialogue. If you notice people who don't tell good stories, they only tell you what happened. They don't bring you in and actually tell you what people said line by line. You get it? So you have to bring people into your story with active dog. Okay. Move on to number five. Okay. Again, and this is part of that is they always tell the story in the moment. I go through the whole entire story as it was happening. So watch. So we get in the car, he says, the kids, you gotta stay pumped up and positive. And no matter how many times the dog gets slender face, just always say, how did I stay? So I'm like, why? He goes, cause it makes you feel better about yourself. You feel good. I said, all right, no, sounds like it makes. So I said, all right. So the penguin drives up the car. We go to this little town up in Westchester. He gets out of the car and he waddles up to the freaking car. And I always try to use humor a little bit, right? And he knocks on the first door and a woman opens the door. Again, a woman opens the door. Not, he walked up the door. A woman opened the door and she, I said, and she opens the door and he says, hi, my name's Elliot Slam. And he goes, have a nice day, right? And I bring you and I start telling, I go through the entire story, blow by blow in the story. I don't tell it as if it happened eight years ago. I start off in the past tense, but I jump in the middle and I tell you the story in the present tense as it's happening now. Okay. Next. Here's the big one. Now, along with that, the devil is in the details. Here's a big one. People that don't tell good stories tend to under describe what was actually happening. They don't tell you what someone was wearing, what people looked like, what the surroundings were like. They skim over it. Oh, we went to this beautiful house. No. We walked to this gigantic red brick house with gigantic Corinthian columns rising up on either side. The door was so fucking big, I felt like the Wizard of Oz. I mean, I see the Wizard. That's what I felt. That's how big this fucking house was. The details. It wasn't a big house. It was the biggest house. The door, I felt like this. So how I feel and how I felt. I knock on this gigantic copper door with fucking this ornate columns on either side. I'm looking at this like a fucking Greek temple. Okay. The neighborhood. I felt this big. It was the craziest thing ever. The details. I walk in. We look in the, I walk inside my yard. It's the yard story, right? I walk in this. There they are. There's eight people. They're sitting on a leopard print carpet. They have a thick dark rope. They probably don't think of the dark rope and there's decorative plates. Vases are falling. I describe. I don't just say, and I walked in there. We're sitting there. It was mayhem. That's not a story. It was disaster. The waves are so big. People were freaking out. I don't say people were afraid. And my wife turns to me and she flashes me this fucking look with her blue eyes. She says, you fucking killed us. Details is they leave out the details. When you write, when you read a really good, a well written book, they describe a location to the point where you feel like you're in the location. The best person I've ever seen was Tom Wolf. Tom Wolf, have you ever read Tom Wolf? He writes so well, he can describe a refrigerator and make it interesting. Like the inside of an industrial refrigerator and he actually makes it like, oh my God, I knew a refrigerator could be interesting. So what the point is, is that you can do this. But I think most people feel like if I, people will get bored if I tell them what it was really like. I want to move quicker in my story. I don't want to get bored with the details. No. The devil is in the details. Whenever I, so Tom, let's go back to the story with Elliot. So we go, here's how I really tell the story. All right, so we go up to Westchester, we get out of this truck. Elliot, he waddles up with his fucking stick in his ass, he waddles up to this fucking door. He goes like this, he goes, watch. He knocks, he gives you that, that, that confident knock. His chest is popped out and the woman opens the door. She's a kind looking woman and he says, hi, my name's Elliot. I have to live a meeting seat with two neighbors. I have a slam and he goes, have a nice day. And he turns to me, Elliot, he goes, that's fine, don't worry about it. He goes right down the cobblestone, he walks back, waddles up to the next door. Knock, knock. I tell you blow by blow. I don't just say to you, he knocked on five doors and all the people slam the door in their face. No one wanted the food. That's not a story. But that's what most people will do when they tell a story. They won't actually take you through the nitty gritty of what happened because they think that's what's boring. That's not what's boring. What's boring is when you tell a story with poor tonality. When you don't spend enough time describing the locations, you get it. In other words, our intuition very often of what we think makes something boring is not what makes it boring. What you think might make it boring is actually what makes the story interesting. Provided that you do all the other things down the road, we study with the straight line, we study tonality. We study body language. Of course, those things matter. So if you can't tell a story in the tonality of like the teacher from Ferris Buell's Day Off, remember that teacher? Open up your book to page four of the smooth hall. You'll put people to fucking sleep like that. So obviously things like tonality and body language matter. But in terms of the pure strategy of telling a story, let's move on now to the next one. The point is that when you're telling a story, remember, let's refresh and go back to being one more time here. While I plug it back in, I want to make sure I don't miss a step. So number one is you start off, let's do it again. Frame a point. What I'd like you to do right now, everyone is watching this, take 15 seconds here, all right? And everyone here, figure out a story. What's a story that you frequently tell in business? Let's make it a business. Something that you tell people, a very common sort of illustrates how great your business is or what service you provide. A story you tell, a story you're out of customers or anything. It's a very typical story. And just think about what that story is right now. Okay? And think about how you actually frame that point. Notice, for me, in business, I have very clear launching points to my stories. They come at the same time every time. Let me tell you a story about Eastman Kodak. I wrote the Eastman Kodak script. I know when I tell my stories, they don't happen at random times. They happen at specific times. Okay? So that's number one. Frame your point correctly. Number two. Okay? Is your patent interrupt. What is your patent interrupt for your story? In other words, you've got to start your story with something that is not expected. Something that sort of just makes people say, what? Like, I wasn't expecting to hear that. That's what zeroes someone's attention into you. In other words, typically what happens is, remember, let's look at how real life works. We all move through the world, right? And we have a lot of things in our mind, right? And someone's talking to us, and we're kind of paying attention. Maybe like 60% of our attention. And the other 40% of the time that we forgot to do this. They very seldom do you have someone's complete attention. They listen to you, and they look at you, and they think you have five other things as they look at you. And you're probably all doing the same thing. People, I tell the human brains, oh, they might be, I focused on you, and my brain wanders at the same time. The purpose of a pattern interrupt is by saying something that's out of the ordinary or outlandish. It makes the person say, what? And you grab 100% of their attention. That's the importance of a pattern interrupt, okay? Next step, number three, is tell a personal story. So, and again, the lesson here is that I want you to go in your own life as a salesperson, as a business owner. What stories are you telling? You definitely are. If you're not, you have to start telling stories to illustrate your goods, your services, what you do, right? Tell them about customers. But what stories are you telling, right? What points are you framing, right? And make sure that the story is not really a personal story. If you're telling someone else's story, start telling your own story. That's the thing, you were involved in. Seem through your own two eyes. That's the key to successful storytelling. Don't tell someone else's story. Next step in the process is you want to use active dialogue to tell the story. I mean, you don't just tell a story that all happened and this person went and did that, and then they went and did there, and then they screamed, and this person, this person, it's no. They turned to me and said, X, Y, Z. And the second that you do that, you own the other person's attention. When you add dialogue into your story, that's the point where people really become engaged because you're bringing it to life then. So always tell your story using dialogue and two, you tell your story in the moment. You don't tell the story past tense. It happened way back when. Now let's go to the next step, which is you want to make sure that you include details. Describe the locations. Describe what the people looked like. Describe how they walk, how they act, where they went. So don't just, you know, people gloss over those details and those details will really bring a story to life. Okay? So make sure you, again, you don't just, I think that the problem that we have with this is that most people think, if I tell too many details, I'll be boring. It's the opposite. It's the details of the story, the color, the richness, the texture. That's what makes the story interesting. Without that, the story is hollow. It's like the difference, you know, when you walk into like a town or those Hollywood like, you know, where there's no, there's just a storefront. There's nothing behind it, right? It lacks the same feel and depth and texture of when something's fully built out. You want to fully build out your locations for people or else they don't. It's about getting them emotionally connected to what you do. Okay? Now, next step, next step, number seven is you want to bring the story back around to the lesson. And here's the point. In other words, the whole point is that you do this whole thing. You frame the point. You use all the stuff. You tell your story. But it has to then circle back down so you essentially do what's called you close a loop. So watch the story now with the penguin. You're not in this one. That's all right. Slam, slam, slam. And he still haven't I say, haven't I say, stick tight in his ass. All positive, right? But then somewhere around 1230, I noticed something strange. He gets out of the truck and his shoulder slump over his head comes down and he starts walking. The stick falls out of his ass. He's walking all slow. And instead of that big confident, you know, knock knock, he's like, we call it the non-closers knock. He's almost like scared to knock on the door. The guy dials the telephone so slow because he doesn't want to hear the next rejection, right? And he barely knocks. And the door opens up. And watch what I do. I always and the door opens up present tense. I always notice just notice the things I do. The door opens up and a kind looking woman comes out. She's gonna help you and he goes, you wouldn't want any filet mignon strip ball lobstails. I mean, the last 20 people all slam the door. They said no. So I'm assuming you don't want either, right? Now he didn't say those words. He didn't have to. They oozed out of every pore of his body. He lost his state of certainty because of rejection after rejection. He's still so positive, but enough rejection and his shoulders. They were just so obvious. And of course that woman didn't buy and for the rest of the day, no one bought as well. He didn't sell a single box of meat. And I will never, you know, you don't remember everything that you see or go through, but I'll never forget that. I'll never forget that feeling of watching someone lose their state of certainty. And I swore to myself at that moment, I would never let it happen to me. Next day I get my own truck, right? So I drive up to that first big door, right? And then I don't want to finish my story, right? By saying how I first asked all of the company, the director, blah, blah, blah, right? And then that's what it's about. And then I end the story now. But the point is this. What I learned from that and what I've instilled in every salesperson. I've ever trained and every salesperson I've managed is that you cannot close if you are not in a state of certainty. Now, back in the days of Stratton, I think call it state management. We call it getting negative or not being positive, right? Now scientifically, right, it's state management. All I can say to this is fucking crucial. Here's my lesson. You must learn to manage your state. And that is why the process I'm about to teach you is so crucial to your success. Because without the ability to manage your state, you cannot access the straight line at the highest level. Does that make sense to everyone? And now this they're all, yeah, of course it does. Because I use the story to reinforce a point. So remember, I tell this whole story, but I don't just tell it for the sake of it. I loop back and I always, it's called I close my loop. I close my loop by telling you and the moral of the story is, and that's why you can't do this. And then they say, now I see why you told me the story. You didn't tell it to waste my time or to divert it made sense. So you, a story, if you tell the wrong story, you know, it's just like it's not congruent. You can't do it. You have to have a story that actually, at the end of the day, and that's why I told you the story and that's the moral. So it's not like so much the moral of the story is like, the moral of the story is evident, but, and that's why I told you. That's what it means to close the loop. Okay. There's one step I want to do before I start doing the Q and A here. Okay. And that is next. I mentioned this casually as I was telling you, and it's definitely so important, but you need to always recreate the tonality. Of the moment. In other words, the stories don't work without the right tonalities. That's why the entire straight line always comes into play. It's a communication strategy. All right. So in other words, when I'm telling you the story, I don't just say, and Elliot walked through the door and said, hi ma'am, do you want me to, I'm like, hi, you want me to lay me on. I tried it. I always create. He looks because listen, the key is he's got a prompt. I don't say he said to me the key was to be pumped in positive. You'd say, all right, I would give a fuck. Right. But instead I say, listen, the key, you got to be pumped in positive. I do my best to recreate Elliot's tonality. When you know who the best in the world this was Robin Williams. Remember Robin Williams? Robin Williams would tell a story and he would almost be dying over how he would be imitated old lady or a young guy or Eddie Murphy's great this Chris Rock is great at it. You know, they have this way and this is what great comedians do. They're storytellers. They tell you a story in a way where they don't have to sound like a woman, but you sound like the character of what that person just brings little story. So the point is, is that the ability to tell a great story, not only is it allowed you to sell more effectively, because for instance, when I tell you, like, I'm going to try to create certainty for like a stop, right? So I could tell you, Eastman Kodak back in the day of my pitch many years ago, right? $140, right? Stocks 45, right? That's an arithmetic. Okay, it makes sense. Take the company, break it up. You'll have 120 hours a share. The stock's 45. Okay, that's great too. That's mad. So a logic. I said, but remember back in 1984 when the plant exploded in Bhopal, India, Union Carbide, right? And 13,000 Indians died. Well, guess what happened? The next day the stock of Union Carbide dropped from 35 down to four, because they got hit with lawsuits from everybody, right? Five years later, when those lawsuits settled, the stock of Union Carbide ran to $68 a share, a massive winner. Texaco got sued by Penzo for patenting, remember that? Okay, the day that lawsuit was filed, the stock of Texaco dropped from 61 down, I'm making up numbers there, down to 12. The day that lawsuit settled, the stock ran to $130, 400% gain. US Steel was sued by Nipon Steel for patent infringement. That stock went to $6 a share. What happened to the day that lawsuit settled? The stock ran from 6 to 110. Right now, Eastman Kodak is being sued by Polar for $1 billion. They have that much cash on their balance sheet waiting to settle the case. Let me ask you a question. What do you think is going to happen to the price of Kodak when the lawsuit gets settled and all the institutions go pouring back into the stock now? Where is the stock going to go? And they'll say, well, obviously it's going to go like this. Exactly. You tell a story. I tell you a story about a similar situation that's a perfect, let me tell you about a customer I had. They were in a situation like yours. You know, the old way of handling objections, they feel, felt, found. I had a customer who felt like you did, right? Okay. And let me tell you what I found. It doesn't work, but in the context of looping, when you tell us, let me tell you about a customer I had to create certainty, tell them a story using the technique I taught you and watch what happens. People become so certain when they hear a story because the human brain, we've been, our DNA, we are wired to be able to extrapolate from a story to our own life because that's how we learned back in the caveman days. Nothing connects to your prospects more or to just the people you want to get into report and telling a story. But make sure the story starts, has a point that you frame, you always frame first. It's the same thing. It's like when you go to a, you're never going to hit this to show, right? Before you even walk in the arena outside, they'll be like, beware of strange things that will happen. They're telling you how you're going to feel. You're going to love this. This is going to be freaked you out. This is going to be a mind bending experience. You haven't experienced it. They're telling you what to expect. They pre frame you. That's why you always pre frame. Tell someone what the point is that you're trying to make. It gives context to your story. If you just tell a story like, what the fuck is this guy talking about? Always have a frame first. So you set the frame, you tell the story and then close your loop at the end and bring it back to the moral and then continue on with where you are in the conversation. That's how you tell a story in a business setting and it works like magic. It's an integral part of the straight line and it also requires you to use other parts of the straight line in the sense that you need to be using the right tonality, right, and so forth. Remember when I said either it was in a white boy Wednesday or maybe in the last couple of which is I said about the law of attraction. It's real, but people bastardize it because they don't understand is that yes, there is a law of attraction and yes, it fucking works like magic, but not when you sit on your fucking couch. It happens when you take action and move towards the things that you want. The reason the law of attraction works for these guys is they got up off their asses. They got in a plane and flew down somewhere. They went towards what they wanted. When you get into the vicinity of what you want, then you can attract it. You will never attract shit into your life by wishing it. They, what they're saying is, is what he's really saying is, is that we take action. We don't live in fear. We move towards the things that we want. And because of that, we attract success into our life. And we also, as you can tell here, they paid a lot of money to be here. Something good has always happened. And when we got the opportunity to be here with you, which is, I remember seeing the movie and learning about it and watching a hundred YouTube videos and, you know, the whole thing, I was like, unbelievable human being, right? And then to get the opportunity to be here, I was never going to say no. Who the hell knows what's going to lead? Here's the thing though. I want to focus on one thing. His story. He told the story, right? Let me tell you a couple of tips here. You can tell the story better, right? One thing I left out of my storytelling is that a big part of telling a great story is when do you enter into the story? The biggest mistake people make when they tell a story is they have a habit of entering into the story too early. In other words, what is your entry point into a story? When you're telling a story, it says, okay, I tell a story about the meat and seafood business, right? And I have to go back and say, all started when my mother told me about it. I mentioned dental school for a second, but I moved past it. And I entered the story when I walked into the warehouse and they put me on a truck. That's where I start my story. I could have started the story way before that. People have a tendency to enter the story at a point way too early. What you don't want to do is you want to almost, you can enter your stories in the middle, right before the lesson happens. Tell a rich story, but about the shortest time possible. So when a story drags on, it's very much because it's almost always because someone entered into the story too early, that you could have started three quarters of the way in and got to the same point. So tell a richer story about a shorter period of time, not a summary of events over a long period of time, a very detailed analysis of a short period of time. I'll give you one more example, helicopter story, right? And then what I'm going to do here is I'm going to do another Q&A. We'll do a special Q&A this week, everybody, on Wednesday. Am I around Wednesday? When do we leave? Okay, we're going to do a Tuesday night. We'll do another Q&A because I want to end this Q&A after this because we're still in Chicago. We'll do another Q&A, especially one of everyone on. So I have to answer the rest. Yeah, so many questions. I'll answer them all on Tuesday, okay? You can tune in on Tuesday. We'll do it at 5 p.m. Pacific stand time. But let me just tell you one more story here because I think it's important because he mentioned helicopters. And I want to explain to you, and he started chiming in about what was going on, right? So let me just tell you, I told the story, I think a couple of weeks ago, in a daily motivation, maybe some of you have seen it. I told the story in the context of about, very similar to about how people have a tendency to focus on what they're afraid of. And if you focus on what you're afraid of, where you don't want to go, you will crash into that. What these guys have become masters of and why they have the fastest growing company in the country for a few years now in their industry, in the country. They have wild access to a hundred million dollar plus revenue, very successful, self-made guys, okay? So they're being modest, okay? They're a huge company. They do amazing, right? And they've mastered the art of not focusing on what they're afraid of, which is probably what you're doing if you're not succeeding in life. Human nature is the focus, you know, you see, it's the whole story. It's always some fucking poor schnook rich bastard in a Ferrari, alright? He's driving in upstate New York, and there's a hundred yards of open field this way, a hundred yards of open field that way, and you see this guy in his fucking Ferrari, he smashes into the tree. You're like, why the fuck did he, he had a hundred yards here, why did he smash into the tree? You know what it was? Because you look back 75 yards and you saw skid marks. Because when he went into his skid and lost control of his beloved Ferrari, he's like, oh no, not the tree, not the tree! He fucked, boom, into the tree. Because he focused on that one fucking thing he didn't want to crash into. Please, not the tree, no, not the tree! This way! You're, you're, where you focus on, that's where your body takes you. That's where the law of attraction takes you. It's where nature takes you. Now you believe in, you have to resist the urge to focus on the negative. We want as, as, as boxer, and you're trained to be a boxer, which I'm not, but I know what it's about though, so I'm taking lessons. The first thing it teach you is you can't, you, you, you can't close your eyes because you don't want, you know, it's like, it's like, the worst thing that happens to a boxer is to get blindsided by a punch. A blindsided punch is what knocks you out because you don't brace for it. We want to look, so we want to see what we're afraid of, so that's that in two minutes you're going to focus on that, right? What happens is you focus on what you fear and you end up going right into it. When I learned to fly a helicopter, I told you this story, right? We have a process called auto rotation. It's what you do when you're up at altitude. If the engine dies, you can land a chopper without crashing. They show you how to do it. You spool down the engine, this is how you train for it, right? You go into a controlled 45 degree angle dive and when you get 50 feet above the ground, you flare back on the stick and it bleeds up the energy, right? And they say, focus ahead on the runway and you'll skid to a landing. So I did that. I was, I trained to that. I go up to altitude with the pilot next to me, right? He spools down the engine, right? And I go into my dive, right? I'm bleeding off airspeed. I get to 50 feet. I pull back on my stick and I'm like, holy shit. Well, and I'm looking at the ground because I'm scared that I'm going to crash in and I start crashing into the ground. He pushes my chip, grabs the stick, pulls it back and the helicopter glides, skids to a safe landing, right? And I couldn't believe this. I'm like, fuck, he told me not to focus down, but I still focused on the ground because when we're in that skid, in that dive, when we're careening out of control in life, when we have a bad turn of business, we focus on our worst fears happening. And because we focus on that, that's what we crash into. And I was like, fuck, let me do it again. Next time we go up to altitude, right? A thousand feet, he turns off the engine, right? We start diving down. We get to 50 feet. And I say, this time I'm not going to do it. So I pull back, show it up, my fucking eyes look down. I start skid, he just kind of yanks my chin up, all right? I pull back on the stick and I go to a safe landing. I couldn't believe it. Did it again. Third time. Turn off the engine. I start gliding down, except this time I didn't look down. I looked straight ahead. I resisted the urge to look down. I pulled back and I went to a safe landing. Why? Because through practice, I conditioned a new pattern. You get it? You have to learn just by practice. That's why the daily motivations, why do you think every day I'm in your ear? Because every day saying, don't fall into the trap. Don't get negative. Don't do that. Every different message you say, don't focus on what's wrong. Focus on what's right. Teach you better skills. Make sure that you feel confident about that. You get it and after a while, just by sheer habit, you start looking ahead to where you want to go. What they've done, why they're so successful, is they've mastered the art of not saying no to things they should say yes to. Most people, you give them an amazing opportunity and they'll say, oh, it's too far. It's inconvenient. What's the cost bet? And they say, you know what? No. We know we're great at what we do in business. We know that we can make, we know that we know the business side of shit. So now it's just a matter of putting ourselves in positions to find opportunity. That's what it's about. It's getting yourself in positions to seize opportunity. That's what they've mastered. So I want you to think about your own life right now in the context of everything you do. You know, there's a funny movie, Jim Carrey, in the Yes Person. Yes, man. Yes, man.
